beats.narration_text: Aloha — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to create a skill, add a skill to a plugin, write a new skill, improve a skill description, or organize skill content — the Skill Development skill fires. A skill is a self-contained package: a SKILL.md file with YAML frontmatter and a markdown body, plus optional bundled resources — scripts for deterministic code, references for documentation loaded as needed, and assets for output files. Progressive disclosure: skill metadata is always in context, the SKILL.md body loads when the skill triggers, and bundled resources load only when Claude needs them. Let me walk you through the anatomy, the creation process, and where this skill gets it right and where it bites.

props.command: Create a skill for my plugin that handles PDF rotation tasks.
output: SKILL.md: YAML frontmatter (name, description) + imperative body (1,500-2,000 words)
3 resource types: scripts/ (code) · references/ (docs) · assets/ (output files)
Progressive disclosure: metadata always → body on trigger → resources as needed

beats.narration_text: Progressive disclosure works in three levels. Level one: skill metadata — name and description — always loaded in context at roughly 100 words. The description is critical: it determines when Claude activates the skill. Use third-person format with specific trigger phrases: this skill should be used when the user asks to create a hook, add a PreToolUse hook, implement prompt-based hooks. Not vague: use this skill for hook guidance. Level two: the SKILL.md body loads when the skill triggers — keep it under five thousand words, targeting fifteen hundred to two thousand words. Level three: bundled resources load only when Claude determines they're needed — no word limit because scripts can execute without being read into context. The anatomy: SKILL.md has YAML frontmatter with name and description required, and a markdown body with core procedures in imperative form. Scripts in scripts/ are for code that would be rewritten repeatedly — a rotate_pdf.py script for a PDF skill. References in references/ are for documentation Claude should read while working — schemas, API specs, company policies, detailed workflow guides. Keep SKILL.md lean by moving detailed content to references/. Assets in assets/ are files used in the output — templates, images, boilerplate — not loaded into context, just available for Claude to copy or reference.

props.sparkLine: Metadata triggers. Body guides. Resources load as needed. Keep each level lean.

beats.narration_text: The creation process has six steps. One: understand the skill — identify concrete examples of how it will be used, ask what a user would say to trigger it. Two: plan resources — for each example, ask what would be rewritten every time and what could be stored as a script, reference, or asset. Three: create the structure — the skill directory under skills/ with SKILL.md and any resource subdirectories. Four: edit — start with resources, then write SKILL.md last, ensuring the body is lean and references the resources you created. Five: validate — check structure, description quality, writing style, progressive disclosure. Six: iterate — use the skill on real tasks, notice inefficiencies, update and test. Four writing rules. Rule one: description is third person with specific phrases — this skill should be used when the user asks to. Not: use this skill when. Rule two: body is imperative form — start by reading the configuration file, not you should start by reading. Rule three: keep SKILL.md lean — target fifteen hundred to two thousand words, move detailed content to references/. Rule four: always reference your resources — if you created references/patterns.md but SKILL.md doesn't mention it, Claude doesn't know to look for it.

props.sparkLine: Third-person triggers. Imperative body. Lean SKILL.md. Reference everything.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. Skill Development gets progressive disclosure right: the three-level loading system — metadata always, body on trigger, resources as needed — is clearly explained and the word limits for each level are concrete. Description quality criteria are precise: third person, specific trigger phrases, concrete scenarios — with explicit before-and-after examples. The imperative form requirement is enforced with side-by-side bad and good examples. The references file design rationale — lean SKILL.md plus detailed content in references — is clearly motivated by context efficiency. And the four mistakes section with bad-good pairs for each makes anti-patterns immediately recognizable. Where it bites: the six-step creation process is thorough but the Understand step has no systematic discovery technique beyond asking questions — no trigger-phrase analysis framework, no user-intent matrix. The trigger mechanism is described but never explained — does Claude Code pattern-match the description or use language model judgment? This matters enormously for how to write effective descriptions. The fifteen hundred to two thousand word target is a recommendation, not a hard limit, and SKILL.md above three thousand words has no warning path — the 5k ceiling appears without a graceful degradation story. The skill-reviewer agent is mentioned as a validation step but how to invoke it and what it actually checks is described only vaguely. And the progressive disclosure claim — references load as needed — implies an autonomous mechanism but the loading trigger is not documented.

props.sparkLine: Disclosure model clear. Trigger mechanism: explain how it actually decides.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Skill Development fires for creating skills, writing SKILL.md, improving descriptions, or organizing skill content. Anatomy: SKILL.md (frontmatter name+description, lean body) + scripts/ (code) + references/ (docs) + assets/ (output). Progressive disclosure: metadata always → body on trigger → resources as needed. Description: third person + specific phrases. Body: imperative form + 1,500-2,000 words + reference all resources. 6-step process: understand → plan → create → edit → validate → iterate. Gaps: trigger mechanism not explained; understand step lacks systematic discovery technique; skill-reviewer agent invocation underdocumented; reference loading trigger implied but not documented.

artifactLines: Anatomy: SKILL.md (name+description fm, lean body) + scripts/ · references/ · assets/
3-level loading: metadata always · body on trigger · resources as needed
Description: third person + specific trigger phrases ("this skill should be used when...")
Body: imperative form · 1,500-2,000 words · always reference supporting files
6-step: understand → plan → create → edit → validate → iterate
Gaps: trigger mechanism unexplained · understand step no framework · reviewer vague

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session. Paste this: Create a skill for my plugin called pdf-editor. The skill should handle rotating PDFs and converting PDF pages to images. Watch four things: first, does Claude write the description in third person with specific trigger phrases — this skill should be used when the user asks to rotate a PDF, convert PDF pages, or similar? Second, does the SKILL.md body use imperative form — process the file with rotate_pdf.py — not you should process the file? Third, does the body stay under two thousand words and reference a scripts directory for the rotate_pdf.py implementation rather than embedding the full script inline? And fourth, does the SKILL.md body explicitly reference any scripts/references/assets files it creates, so Claude knows to look for them? If the description says use this skill for PDF tasks without trigger phrases — it will not activate reliably. If the script is embedded in SKILL.md rather than in scripts/ — progressive disclosure is broken. Those are your gates.

props.command: Create a skill for my plugin called pdf-editor that handles rotating PDFs and converting pages to images.
props.runningText: Watch: third-person description? imperative body? lean SKILL.md? scripts/ referenced?
output: Expected: description says 'This skill should be used when the user asks to rotate a PDF...'
Expected: body imperative form · under 2k words · scripts/rotate_pdf.py referenced
Red flag: 'Use this skill for PDF' · script embedded in SKILL.md · no resource references

beats.narration_text: Skill Development. Liam, in for Bear.
